title: Search in 2D matrix (row/col sorted)
description: Search element in a sorted 2D matrix using binary search

examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize i=0 (first row). Row 0 has elements [1, 3, 5]. Initialize j=0 (first column). Check matrix[0][0] = 1 vs target 9 → not equal. Increment j=1. Check matrix[0][1] = 3 vs 9 → not equal. Increment j=2. Check matrix[0][2] = 5 vs 9 → not equal. End of row 0. Step 2: Increment i=1 (second row). Row 1 has elements [7, 9, 11]. Initialize j=0. Check matrix[1][0] = 7 vs 9 → not equal. Increment j=1. Check matrix[1][1] = 9 vs 9 → equal! Return True immediately.

Step 1: i=0, row [2, 4]. j=0: matrix[0][0]=2 vs 5 → no match. j=1: matrix[0][1]=4 vs 5 → no match. Step 2: i=1, row [6, 8]. j=0: matrix[1][0]=6 vs 5 → no match. j=1: matrix[1][1]=8 vs 5 → no match. Step 3: All elements checked (2, 4, 6, 8). None equal 5. Return False.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(m*n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through every element in the 2D matrix and check if it matches the target. This works for any matrix regardless of sorting properties.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Search in 2D Matrix
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of brute force search through every element in a 2D matrix to find a target value.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to searching in a 2D matrix is the most straightforward method that works regardless of the matrix's sorting properties. The intuition is simple: since we need to determine if a target element exists anywhere in the matrix, we can systematically examine every single element until we either find the target or exhaust all possibilities. This approach treats the 2D matrix as a collection of elements where position doesn't matter for the search logic—we just need to check each one. The algorithm works by using nested loops: the outer loop iterates through each row, and the inner loop iterates through each column within that row. For each element at position (i, j), we compare it with the target value. If we find a match, we return True (or the position, depending on the problem variant). If we complete all iterations without finding a match, we return False. This method is guaranteed to find the target if it exists because it examines every possible location. The time complexity is O(m*n) where m is the number of rows and n is the number of columns, as we visit each of the m*n elements exactly once in the worst case. The space complexity is O(1) since we only use a constant amount of extra space for loop variables and comparisons. This connects to the broader problem of search algorithms by demonstrating the baseline, exhaustive approach against which more optimized methods (like binary search on sorted matrices) can be compared. Common mistakes include: 1) Off-by-one errors in loop boundaries (e.g., using < instead of <= for 1-indexed systems), 2) Returning too early without checking all elements, 3) Confusing row and column indices in access patterns. To avoid these, always verify loop ranges match the matrix dimensions (0 to m-1 for rows, 0 to n-1 for columns in 0-indexing) and ensure the return logic covers all cases. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def search_matrix(matrix, target): for i in range(len(matrix)): for j in range(len(matrix[0])): if matrix[i][j] == target: return True return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize i=0 (first row). Row 0 has elements [1, 3, 5]. Initialize j=0 (first column). Check matrix[0][0] = 1 vs target 9 → not equal. Increment j=1. Check matrix[0][1] = 3 vs 9 → not equal. Increment j=2. Check matrix[0][2] = 5 vs 9 → not equal. End of row 0. Step 2: Increment i=1 (second row). Row 1 has elements [7, 9, 11]. Initialize j=0. Check matrix[1][0] = 7 vs 9 → not equal. Increment j=1. Check matrix[1][1] = 9 vs 9 → equal! Return True immediately.

Step 1: i=0, row [2, 4]. j=0: matrix[0][0]=2 vs 5 → no match. j=1: matrix[0][1]=4 vs 5 → no match. Step 2: i=1, row [6, 8]. j=0: matrix[1][0]=6 vs 5 → no match. j=1: matrix[1][1]=8 vs 5 → no match. Step 3: All elements checked (2, 4, 6, 8). None equal 5. Return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Iterating Through All Elements in a 2D Matrix
ladders.desc: The nested loop structure that visits every cell in a 2D matrix systematically.
ladders.explanation: The key sub-routine in brute force search is the nested loop iteration that accesses every element in the 2D matrix exactly once. The intuition is that to examine all elements, we need to traverse the matrix in a structured manner, typically row by row (row-major order) or column by column. This works because a 2D matrix can be viewed as a grid where each element has a unique row and column index, and by varying these indices across their full ranges, we can cover all positions. The algorithm uses two loops: an outer loop for rows (index i from 0 to m-1) and an inner loop for columns (index j from 0 to n-1). For each combination of i and j, we access the element at matrix[i][j]. This ensures we visit all m*n cells without repetition or omission. The time complexity is O(m*n) as we perform constant work per element. The space complexity is O(1) since we only store loop counters. This feeds into the full problem (L0) by providing the mechanism to check each element against the target; in L0, we add a comparison operation inside the inner loop. Common mistakes include: 1) Incorrect loop bounds leading to index out-of-range errors, 2) Using the same variable for both loops, 3) Assuming the matrix is square (m == n). To avoid these, always use len(matrix) for rows and len(matrix[0]) for columns in 0-indexed languages, and ensure inner loop resets j for each row. Real code pattern: for i in range(rows): for j in range(cols): element = matrix[i][j] (then process element).

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0 (row 0). j=0: access matrix[0][0]=1. j=1: access matrix[0][1]=2. Step 2: i=1 (row 1). j=0: access matrix[1][0]=3. j=1: access matrix[1][1]=4. Done.
Step 1: i=0 (only row). j=0: access matrix[0][0]=5. j=1: access matrix[0][1]=6. j=2: access matrix[0][2]=7. Done.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Element Comparison in a Loop
ladders.desc: Checking if a given element equals a target value during iteration.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of brute force search is the comparison operation that determines if the current element matches the target. The intuition is that during iteration through the matrix, we need to test each element against the target to decide if we've found it. This works because equality comparison is a fundamental operation that yields True if the element and target have the same value, and False otherwise. The algorithm involves, within the nested loops, an if statement: if matrix[i][j] == target, then we can take action (e.g., return True). This comparison is performed for each element until a match is found or all elements are exhausted. The time complexity per comparison is O(1), and in the context of the full iteration, it contributes to the overall O(m*n) time. The space complexity is O(1) as no extra data structures are needed. This feeds into L1 (iteration) by adding the decision-making step; L1 provides the iteration mechanism, and this level adds the comparison to identify the target. Common mistakes include: 1) Using assignment (=) instead of equality (==), 2) Comparing against the wrong variable, 3) Not handling different data types properly. To avoid these, double-check operators, ensure the target is defined and accessible in scope, and consider type safety if applicable. Real code pattern: if current_element == target: return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check element 3 == 7? False. Step 2: Check element 7 == 7? True. Stop and indicate found.
Step 1: Check 1 == 5? False. Step 2: Check 2 == 5? False. Step 3: Check 3 == 5? False. All checked, not found.

ladders.title: Building Block — Accessing Elements in a 2D List by Indices
ladders.desc: Retrieving a value from a 2D list using row and column indices.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves accessing a specific element in a 2D matrix given its row and column indices. The intuition is that a 2D matrix is typically represented as a list of lists, where the outer list contains rows, and each inner list contains elements in that row. To get an element, we first select the row using the first index, then select the column within that row using the second index. This works because the data structure is indexed hierarchically: matrix[i] gives the i-th row (a list), and matrix[i][j] gives the j-th element in that row. The algorithm is straightforward: element = matrix[row_index][column_index]. This operation has O(1) time complexity as it involves direct indexing, and O(1) space as it retrieves without copying. This feeds into L2 (comparison) by providing the element to compare; L2 uses this accessed value to check against the target. Common mistakes include: 1) Index out of range errors by using indices beyond list sizes, 2) Confusing row and column order, 3) Assuming all rows have the same length without validation. To avoid these, ensure indices are within 0 to len(matrix)-1 for rows and 0 to len(matrix[0])-1 for columns, and remember that matrix[i][j] means row i, column j. Real code pattern: value = matrix[i][j].

examples.input: matrix = [[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]], access element at row 1, column 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Row index 1 refers to second row: [4, 5, 6]. Step 2: Column index 2 refers to third element in that row (0-indexed): 6. So matrix[1][2] = 6.

examples.input: matrix = [[10, 20], [30, 40]], access element at row 0, column 1
examples.trace: Step 1: Row 0 is [10, 20]. Step 2: Column 1 is second element: 20. So matrix[0][1] = 20.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating with a For Loop Over a Range
ladders.desc: Using a for loop to repeat an action a fixed number of times.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using a for loop to iterate over a sequence of numbers, typically generated by a range function. The intuition is that many algorithms require repeating an action multiple times, and a for loop provides a controlled way to do this by executing a block of code for each value in the sequence. This works because the loop variable takes on each value in the range one by one, allowing systematic repetition. For example, for i in range(5): will set i to 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 in order, executing the loop body each time. The algorithm is simple: define the range (start, stop, step), and place the code to repeat inside the loop. The time complexity depends on the number of iterations, often O(n) for n iterations. The space complexity is O(1) for the loop variable. This feeds into L3 (accessing elements) by providing the mechanism to generate indices; in L3, we use loops to produce row and column indices for accessing matrix elements. Common mistakes include: 1) Off-by-one errors (e.g., range(5) gives 0 to 4, not 1 to 5), 2) Modifying the loop variable inside the loop causing unexpected behavior, 3) Using the wrong range bounds. To avoid these, remember that range(stop) goes from 0 to stop-1, and avoid changing the loop variable. Real code pattern: for i in range(n): # do something with i.

examples.input: Iterate i from 0 to 2 and print i
examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, print 0. Step 2: i=1, print 1. Step 3: i=2, print 2. Done.

examples.input: Iterate j from 1 to 3 and compute j*2
examples.trace: Step 1: j=1, compute 1*2=2. Step 2: j=2, compute 2*2=4. Step 3: j=3, compute 3*2=6. Done.

testCases.input: for i in range(3): print i
for k in range(1,4): print k
for x in range(0): print x
for y in range(2): print y*10
for z in range(5): print z+1
for a in range(1,6,2): print a
for b in range(4): print b*b
for d in range(2,5): print d-1
for e in range(10,13): print e

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Variables and Values
ladders.desc: Basic concept of storing and using data in variables.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves understanding what variables are and how they hold values in programming. The intuition is that variables are named containers that store data, such as numbers or other types, which can be used and manipulated in a program. This works because when you assign a value to a variable (e.g., x = 5), the variable x now refers to that value, and you can use x in expressions to represent 5. Variables allow you to work with data dynamically without hard-coding values. For example, in the context of search, we might have a variable target that holds the value we're looking for. The algorithm is simple: declare a variable by giving it a name and assigning a value using the = operator. The time and space complexity are not typically analyzed at this level, as it's a fundamental operation. This feeds into L4 (for loops) because loops often use variables as counters or indices; understanding variables is prerequisite to using loop variables like i in for i in range(n). Common mistakes include: 1) Using a variable before assigning a value, 2) Confusing variable names, 3) Not updating variables when needed. To avoid these, always initialize variables before use, choose clear names, and reassign as necessary. Real code pattern: target = 10 (assign value 10 to variable target).

examples.input: Assign value 3 to variable a, then print a
examples.trace: Step 1: Execute a = 3. Now variable a stores the value 3. Step 2: Print a, which outputs 3.

examples.input: Assign value 7 to variable x, assign value 2 to variable y, then compute x + y
examples.trace: Step 1: x = 7, so x is 7. Step 2: y = 2, so y is 2. Step 3: Compute x + y = 7 + 2 = 9.

approaches.name: Row-wise Binary Search O(m*log n)
approaches.summary: For each row, perform binary search to check if the target exists in that row. This leverages the sorted property within each row but treats rows independently.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Search in Row/Col Sorted Matrix using Row-wise Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete solution searching for target in sorted 2D matrix by applying binary search on each row.
ladders.explanation: This approach leverages the fact that each row is individually sorted, allowing us to treat the matrix as m independent sorted arrays. The intuition is straightforward: since we know the target might be in any row, we must check all rows. For each row, we perform binary search to efficiently determine if the target exists in that row. This works because binary search on a sorted array has O(log n) time complexity, and we repeat it m times (once per row), resulting in O(m log n) overall time. The space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few pointers. Step-by-step: 1. Get dimensions m (rows) and n (cols). 2. For each row i from 0 to m-1: a. Initialize left = 0, right = n-1. b. While left <= right: i. mid = (left + right) // 2. ii. If matrix[i][mid] == target, return True. iii. If matrix[i][mid] < target, left = mid + 1. iv. Else, right = mid - 1. 3. If no match found after all rows, return False. Connection to L1: This uses the binary search subroutine from L1 repeatedly. Common mistakes include not handling empty matrices, incorrect loop bounds, or forgetting to return early upon finding the target. Real code pattern: def searchMatrix(matrix, target): m, n = len(matrix), len(matrix[0]) for i in range(m): left, right = 0, n-1 while left <= right: mid = (left + right) // 2 if matrix[i][mid] == target: return True elif matrix[i][mid] < target: left = mid + 1 else: right = mid - 1 return False

examples.trace: Step 1: m=3, n=4. Row 0: left=0, right=3, mid=1, matrix[0][1]=3 == target → return True.

Step 1: m=3, n=4. Row 0: left=0, right=3, mid=1=3<13→left=2, mid=2=5<13→left=3, mid=3=7<13→left=4>right→end. Row 1: left=0, right=3, mid=1=11<13→left=2, mid=2=16>13→right=1, left=2>right→end. Row 2: left=0, right=3, mid=1=30>13→right=0, mid=0=23>13→right=-1, left=0>right→end. No match → return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search on a Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Implement binary search to find a target in a sorted 1D array.
ladders.explanation: Binary search is an efficient algorithm for finding an element in a sorted array by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half. The intuition is based on the sorted property: if we look at the middle element, we can determine whether the target is in the left or right half, eliminating half of the remaining elements each step. This gives O(log n) time complexity, where n is the array length, and O(1) space. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize left = 0, right = n-1. 2. While left <= right: a. mid = (left + right) // 2. b. If arr[mid] == target, return True (or index). c. If arr[mid] < target, set left = mid + 1 (target is in right half). d. Else, set right = mid - 1 (target is in left half). 3. If loop ends, return False (target not found). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each row in the matrix. Common mistakes include using incorrect loop condition (left < right instead of left <= right), integer overflow in mid calculation (use left + (right-left)//2), or not updating pointers correctly. Real code pattern: def binary_search(arr, target): left, right = 0, len(arr)-1 while left <= right: mid = (left + right) // 2 if arr[mid] == target: return True elif arr[mid] < target: left = mid + 1 else: right = mid - 1 return False

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=3, mid=1, arr[1]=3 == target → return True.
Step 1: left=0, right=3, mid=1=3<4→left=2, mid=2=5>4→right=1, left=2>right→end → return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterating Over Rows and Applying Search
ladders.desc: Loop through each row of a matrix and perform an operation (search) on each row.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the high-level logic of processing a 2D matrix row by row. The intuition is that a matrix can be viewed as a collection of rows, and we need to examine each row independently to find a target. This involves iterating through all rows using a loop and, for each row, executing a search operation. The time complexity is O(m) for the iteration, plus the cost of the search per row. The space complexity is O(1) for loop variables. Step-by-step: 1. Get number of rows m. 2. For i from 0 to m-1: a. Access row i as matrix[i]. b. Perform search on this row (e.g., binary search). c. If found, return early. 3. If not found after all rows, return False. Connection to L1: This uses the binary search from L1 inside the loop. Common mistakes include not handling empty matrices, incorrect indexing, or forgetting to break early upon success. Real code pattern: def search_rows(matrix, target): m = len(matrix) for i in range(m): row = matrix[i] # Perform search on row, e.g., if target in row: return True return False

examples.trace: Step 1: m=3. Row 0: [1,2], target not in row. Row 1: [3,4], target in row → return True.
Step 1: m=3. Row 0: [1,2], no. Row 1: [3,4], no. Row 2: [5,6], no. After all rows → return False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Accessing Elements in a 2D Matrix
ladders.desc: Understand how to navigate and access elements in a 2D array using row and column indices.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental concept of working with 2D matrices in programming. A 2D matrix is typically represented as a list of lists, where each inner list is a row. The intuition is that we use two indices: the first index selects the row, and the second selects the column within that row. For example, matrix[i][j] accesses the element at row i, column j. This is crucial for searching, as we need to examine specific elements. Step-by-step: 1. Matrix dimensions: m rows and n columns. 2. Valid indices: row i from 0 to m-1, column j from 0 to n-1. 3. Access element: value = matrix[i][j]. 4. Iterate: Use nested loops to access all elements. Time complexity for accessing one element is O(1); iterating all elements is O(m*n). Space is O(1) for indices. Connection to L2: This skill is used to access each row and its elements during iteration. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors, confusing row and column order, or misinterpreting matrix structure. Real code pattern: # Access element at row 1, col 2: element = matrix[1][2] # Iterate all elements: for i in range(len(matrix)): for j in range(len(matrix[0])): print(matrix[i][j])

examples.trace: Step 1: matrix[0] is [1,2,3]. Step 2: matrix[0][1] is element at index 1 → 2.

examples.input: matrix = [[10,20],[30,40],[50,60]], iterate and sum all elements

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Numbers and Updating Pointers
ladders.desc: Perform comparisons between numbers and update variables based on conditions.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the elementary operations of comparing values and adjusting pointers or indices accordingly. The intuition is that in algorithms like binary search, we need to decide the next search direction by comparing the current element with the target. This involves using comparison operators (<, >, ==) and then updating pointers (e.g., left, right) to narrow down the search range. These operations are fundamental to control flow in search algorithms. Step-by-step: 1. Compare two numbers: e.g., if a < b, a == b, or a > b. 2. Based on comparison, update a variable: e.g., if a < b, set left = mid + 1. 3. Repeat until a condition is met. Time complexity per operation is O(1), space is O(1). Connection to L3: This is used when accessing matrix elements and deciding search direction. Common mistakes include using wrong comparison operators, not updating pointers correctly, or infinite loops due to incorrect conditions. Real code pattern: # Example in binary search context: mid = (left + right) // 2 if arr[mid] == target: return True elif arr[mid] < target: left = mid + 1 else: right = mid - 1

examples.trace: Step 1: Compare arr[mid]=10 < target=15 → condition true. Step 2: Update left = mid + 1 = 3. right remains 5.
Step 1: Compare arr[mid]=20 > target=15 → condition false (use else). Step 2: Update right = mid - 1 = 1. left remains 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Sorted Arrays and Linear Search
ladders.desc: Learn what a sorted array is and how to search for an element by checking each one sequentially.
ladders.explanation: This level introduces the most basic prerequisite: sorted arrays and linear search. A sorted array is a list of numbers arranged in increasing (or decreasing) order, e.g., [1,3,5,7]. The intuition is that order allows for efficient searches, but we start with the simplest method: linear search, which checks each element one by one. This is foundational because it teaches how to traverse an array and compare elements. Step-by-step: 1. Start at the first element (index 0). 2. Compare current element with target. 3. If equal, return True. 4. If not, move to next element. 5. Repeat until end of array; if not found, return False. Time complexity is O(n) for n elements, space is O(1). Connection to L4: Linear search uses comparisons and pointer updates (moving to next index). Common mistakes include not handling empty arrays, off-by-one errors in loops, or returning too early. Real code pattern: def linear_search(arr, target): for num in arr: if num == target: return True return False

examples.trace: Step 1: Check 2 != 6. Step 2: Check 4 != 6. Step 3: Check 6 == 6 → return True.
Step 1: Check 2 != 5. Step 2: Check 4 != 5. Step 3: Check 6 != 5. Step 4: Check 8 != 5. End of array → return False.

approaches.name: Flatten and Binary Search O(log(m*n))
approaches.summary: Treat the 2D matrix as a single sorted 1D array by mapping indices, then perform a single binary search. This requires careful index mapping between 1D and 2D representations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Search in Row/Col Sorted Matrix via Flattened Binary Search
ladders.desc: Implement complete solution to search for target in sorted 2D matrix by treating it as 1D array and performing binary search with index mapping.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that a 2D matrix with rows and columns sorted in ascending order can be treated as a single sorted 1D array when read row-wise. This allows us to apply binary search directly on the entire matrix. The challenge lies in mapping between 1D indices (used in binary search) and 2D indices (used to access matrix elements). For a matrix with m rows and n columns, the total elements are m*n. We can map 1D index 'mid' to 2D coordinates using: row = mid // n, col = mid % n. This works because each row contains n elements, so integer division gives the row number, and remainder gives the column position within that row. The algorithm proceeds like standard binary search: initialize left=0, right=m*n-1. While left <= right, calculate mid, map to 2D indices, compare matrix[row][col] with target. If equal, return True. If matrix value < target, search right half (left = mid+1). If matrix value > target, search left half (right = mid-1). If loop ends without finding target, return False. Time complexity is O(log(m*n)) since we perform binary search on m*n elements. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach is optimal because the matrix is completely sorted when flattened, and binary search gives logarithmic time. Common mistakes include incorrect index mapping (using m instead of n for modulo), off-by-one errors in bounds, and not handling empty matrices. The connection to L1 is that this level implements the complete solution using the index mapping subroutine from L1.

examples.trace: Matrix has m=3 rows, n=4 columns, total elements=12. Initialize left=0, right=11. Iteration 1: mid=(0+11)//2=5. Map to 2D: row=5//4=1, col=5%4=1. matrix[1][1]=11. 11 > 3, so right=mid-1=4. Iteration 2: mid=(0+4)//2=2. row=2//4=0, col=2%4=2. matrix[0][2]=5. 5 > 3, so right=mid-1=1. Iteration 3: mid=(0+1)//2=0. row=0//4=0, col=0%4=0. matrix[0][0]=1. 1 < 3, so left=mid+1=1. Iteration 4: mid=(1+1)//2=1. row=1//4=0, col=1%4=1. matrix[0][1]=3. 3 == 3, return True.

m=3, n=4, total=12. left=0, right=11. Iteration 1: mid=5, row=1, col=1, value=11. 11 < 13, left=6. Iteration 2: mid=(6+11)//2=8. row=8//4=2, col=8%4=0. matrix[2][0]=23. 23 > 13, right=7. Iteration 3: mid=(6+7)//2=6. row=6//4=1, col=6%4=2. matrix[1][2]=16. 16 > 13, right=5. Now left=6, right=5, loop ends. Return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Index Mapping Between 1D and 2D Representations
ladders.desc: Implement function to convert between 1D array index and 2D matrix coordinates for row-wise flattened representation.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the flattened binary search approach. When we treat a 2D matrix as a 1D array by reading rows sequentially, we need a way to convert between the 1D index used in binary search and the actual 2D row/column indices to access matrix elements. For a matrix with 'n' columns per row, the mapping is: given 1D index 'i', the corresponding 2D coordinates are row = i // n (integer division) and col = i % n (modulo). Conversely, given 2D coordinates (row, col), the 1D index is i = row * n + col. The intuition is that each complete row contains 'n' elements, so the row number is how many full rows fit into the first 'i' elements, and the column is the remainder within the current row. This works because the matrix is stored in row-major order in memory. The algorithm is straightforward: for 1D to 2D mapping, take input i and n, return (i//n, i%n). For 2D to 1D mapping, take row, col, n, return row*n + col. Time complexity is O(1) for both operations. Space complexity is O(1). Common mistakes include using the wrong divisor (using m instead of n), forgetting that indices start at 0, and not handling edge cases like n=0. This feeds into L0 by providing the essential coordinate conversion needed to access matrix elements during binary search. Without this mapping, we cannot implement the flattened approach.

examples.trace: For n=4 columns. To map 1D index 5 to 2D: row = 5 // 4 = 1 (integer division). col = 5 % 4 = 1 (remainder). So 1D index 5 corresponds to matrix[1][1].
For n=4 columns. To map 2D coordinates (2,0) to 1D: i = row * n + col = 2 * 4 + 0 = 8. So matrix[2][0] is at 1D index 8 in the flattened array.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary Search on a Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Implement binary search algorithm to find target in sorted 1D array.
ladders.explanation: Binary search is the fundamental algorithm that enables efficient searching in O(log n) time by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half. The intuition is that in a sorted array, comparing the middle element with the target tells us which half of the array to search next. If the middle element equals the target, we found it. If it's less than the target, the target must be in the right half (since array is sorted ascending). If it's greater, the target must be in the left half. We maintain two pointers: 'left' for the start of current search interval and 'right' for the end. Initially, left=0, right=n-1. While left <= right, calculate mid = (left+right)//2. Compare array[mid] with target. If equal, return True (or index). If array[mid] < target, set left = mid+1 to search right half. If array[mid] > target, set right = mid-1 to search left half. If loop ends without finding target, return False. Time complexity is O(log n) as we halve search space each iteration. Space complexity is O(1). Common mistakes include incorrect termination condition (using < instead of <=), integer overflow in mid calculation (use left+(right-left)//2), and not updating bounds correctly. This is the core logic that L0 adapts by applying binary search to the flattened matrix representation. The key insight for L0 is that the sorted 2D matrix behaves like a sorted 1D array when flattened.

examples.trace: n=12. left=0, right=11. Iteration 1: mid=(0+11)//2=5. arr[5]=11. 11 == 11, return True.

left=0, right=11. Iteration 1: mid=5, arr[5]=11. 11 < 13, left=6. Iteration 2: mid=(6+11)//2=8, arr[8]=23. 23 > 13, right=7. Iteration 3: mid=(6+7)//2=6, arr[6]=16. 16 > 13, right=5. Now left=6, right=5, loop ends. Return False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterative Loop with Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Implement a while loop that adjusts two pointers to narrow down a search interval.
ladders.explanation: This building block teaches how to use two pointers (often called 'left' and 'right') to define a search interval that shrinks iteratively. The intuition is that we start with the entire range (left=start, right=end) and repeatedly move the pointers inward based on some condition until they cross, indicating the search is complete. The core pattern is: while left <= right: calculate mid point, evaluate condition, update either left or right. This pattern is fundamental to binary search but appears in many algorithms. The key is understanding how to update pointers correctly: if the condition suggests searching the right half, set left = mid+1; if searching left half, set right = mid-1. The loop terminates when left > right, meaning the interval is empty. Time complexity depends on how much the interval shrinks each iteration; for binary search it's O(log n). Space complexity is O(1). Common mistakes include infinite loops from incorrect pointer updates, off-by-one errors in termination condition, and not handling empty intervals initially. This feeds into L2 as the mechanical implementation of binary search's iterative process. Mastering this pattern is essential before adding the comparison logic of binary search.

examples.input: start=0, end=11, condition: stop when left>right
examples.trace: Initialize left=0, right=11. While left <= right: (enters loop). Suppose we always set left=mid+1 and right=mid-1 with mid=(left+right)//2. Iteration 1: mid=5, update left=6, right=4? Actually need condition to decide. For demonstration, if we set left=mid+1=6, right=mid-1=4. Now left=6, right=4, condition left<=right? 6<=4 is false, loop ends. So loop ran once.

examples.input: start=0, end=5, simulate binary search for target in [1,2,3,4,5,6]
examples.trace: Array indices 0-5. left=0, right=5. Iteration 1: mid=2, value=3. If target>3, left=3. Iteration 2: left=3, right=5, mid=4, value=5. If target<5, right=3. Iteration 3: left=3, right=3, mid=3, value=4. If target==4, found. Loop shows pointer movement.

testCases.input: start=0, end=2, simulate updates: left=mid+1, right=mid-1
start=0, end=3, condition: always move left to mid+1
start=0, end=4, condition: always move right to mid-1
start=0, end=7, simulate binary search pattern
start=0, end=15, condition: stop after 4 iterations

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division and Modulo
ladders.desc: Understand and apply integer division (//) and modulo (%) operations for index calculations.
ladders.explanation: Integer division and modulo are arithmetic operations essential for index mapping in the flattened matrix approach. Integer division (// in Python) divides two integers and returns the quotient rounded down to the nearest integer. For example, 7 // 3 = 2 because 3 fits into 7 twice with remainder 1. Modulo (%) returns the remainder of division. For example, 7 % 3 = 1. Together, they allow us to break a number into parts based on a divisor. In the context of matrix indexing, if we have a 1D index 'i' and 'n' columns per row, i // n gives the row number (how many complete rows fit into i), and i % n gives the column number (position within the current row). Conversely, row * n + col reconstructs the 1D index. These operations are O(1) time and space. Common mistakes include confusing / (float division) with //, misunderstanding negative number behavior (though indices are non-negative here), and not ensuring divisor is non-zero. This feeds into L1 as the mathematical basis for index mapping. Without proficiency in these operations, implementing the coordinate conversion is error-prone.

examples.trace: Calculate row = 7 // 4. 4 fits into 7 once (since 4*1=4), remainder 3. So row=1. col = 7 % 4 = remainder when 7 divided by 4, which is 3. So col=3. Check: row*n+col = 1*4+3=7, correct.
Calculate i = row * n + col = 2 * 4 + 1 = 8 + 1 = 9. Verify: for i=9, row=9//4=2, col=9%4=1, matches.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: Access elements in 1D and 2D arrays using indices, understanding zero-based indexing.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers how to access elements in arrays using indices. In programming, arrays are data structures that store elements in contiguous memory locations. We use indices (starting from 0) to access specific elements. For a 1D array 'arr' of length n, valid indices are 0 to n-1. Element at index i is accessed as arr[i]. For a 2D matrix 'mat' with m rows and n columns, it's typically represented as a list of lists in Python. Element at row r and column c is accessed as mat[r][c], where r ranges from 0 to m-1 and c from 0 to n-1. Zero-based indexing means the first element is at index 0, not 1. This is crucial for correct calculations. Time complexity for access is O(1) assuming random access memory. Space complexity is O(1) for the access operation. Common mistakes include using indices outside valid range (causing IndexError), confusing row and column order, and forgetting that indices start at 0. This is the prerequisite for L4 because index mapping operations produce indices that must be used to access array elements. Without solid understanding of array indexing, implementing any algorithm involving arrays is impossible.

examples.trace: Array indices: 0->10, 1->20, 2->30, 3->40. To access element at index 2, use arr[2]. This returns the value 30.
Matrix has 2 rows (0 and 1) and 3 columns (0,1,2). Row 1 is the second row: [4,5,6]. Column 2 is the third element in that row. So mat[1][2] accesses row 1, column 2, which is 6.

approaches.name: Staircase Search O(m+n)
approaches.summary: Start from the top-right corner and move left or down based on comparisons with the target. This leverages both row and column sorting to eliminate rows/columns efficiently.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Search in Row/Col Sorted Matrix using Staircase Search
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of staircase search algorithm to find target in sorted 2D matrix
ladders.explanation: The staircase search algorithm provides an optimal O(m+n) solution for searching in a matrix where both rows and columns are sorted in ascending order. The key insight is to start from the top-right corner (or bottom-left) and leverage the sorted properties to eliminate either an entire row or column with each comparison. Starting at matrix[0][n-1], we compare the current element with the target. If they're equal, we've found the target. If current > target, we move left (decrease column index) because all elements in the current column below are larger (due to column sorting). If current < target, we move down (increase row index) because all elements in the current row to the left are smaller (due to row sorting). This creates a staircase-like path through the matrix. The algorithm terminates when we find the target or when indices go out of bounds, indicating the target doesn't exist. Time complexity is O(m+n) since in worst case we traverse from top-right to bottom-left. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use constant extra variables. This approach is more efficient than binary searching each row (O(m log n)) and avoids the complexity of treating the 2D matrix as a 1D array for binary search. Common mistakes include starting from wrong corner, incorrect comparison logic, or not handling edge cases like empty matrix. The connection to L1 is that this full solution implements the complete staircase traversal logic described in the key subroutine.

examples.trace: Start at (0,3) = 11. 11 > 9 → move left to (0,2) = 7. 7 < 9 → move down to (1,2) = 8. 8 < 9 → move down to (2,2) = 9. 9 == 9 → found target. Return true.

Start at (0,3) = 11. 11 < 15 → move down to (1,3) = 12. 12 < 15 → move down to (2,3) = 16. 16 > 15 → move left to (2,2) = 9. 9 < 15 → move down to (3,2) = 14. 14 < 15 → move down to row 4 (out of bounds). Return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Staircase Traversal in Sorted Matrix
ladders.desc: Implement the core staircase movement logic that eliminates rows/columns based on comparisons
ladders.explanation: The staircase traversal subroutine is the heart of the algorithm. Given a sorted 2D matrix and a target, we need to implement the logic that starts at a corner and moves through the matrix by comparing elements and deciding whether to move left or down. The intuition is that by starting at the top-right corner, we're at a position where moving left gives us smaller values (since rows are sorted left to right) and moving down gives us larger values (since columns are sorted top to bottom). This creates a decision boundary: if current element > target, all elements below in this column are also > target (column sorted), so we eliminate the entire column by moving left. If current element < target, all elements to the left in this row are also < target (row sorted), so we eliminate the entire row by moving down. The algorithm maintains row and column indices, updates them based on comparisons, and continues until either finding the target or going out of bounds. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(m+n) in worst case. Space is O(1). Connection to L2: This implements the core comparison and movement logic described in the core insight. Common mistakes include moving in wrong direction, not updating indices correctly, or infinite loops when target is not present. The pseudocode pattern: initialize row=0, col=n-1; while row<m and col>=0: if matrix[row][col]==target: return true; elif matrix[row][col]>target: col-=1; else: row+=1; return false.

examples.trace: Start at (0,2)=5. 5>4 → move left to (0,1)=3. 3<4 → move down to (1,1)=4. 4==4 → found target.
Start at (0,2)=5. 5<10 → move down to (1,2)=6. 6<10 → move down to (2,2)=9. 9<10 → move down to row 3 (out of bounds). Target not found.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Leveraging Row and Column Sorting for Elimination
ladders.desc: Understand how sorted properties allow eliminating entire rows/columns with single comparisons
ladders.explanation: The core insight of staircase search is that the sorted properties of both rows and columns create elimination opportunities that binary search in 1D arrays doesn't provide. In a matrix where each row is sorted left-to-right and each column is sorted top-to-bottom, every element has the property that all elements to its right in the same row are larger, and all elements below it in the same column are larger. Conversely, all elements to its left are smaller, and all elements above are smaller. When we compare an element with our target, we can make definitive statements about entire regions of the matrix. If element > target, then EVERY element in the same column below it is also > target (due to column sorting), so we can eliminate that entire column from consideration. If element < target, then EVERY element in the same row to the left is also < target (due to row sorting), so we can eliminate that entire row. This is why starting at a corner works optimally: at top-right corner, we have the maximum elimination power - we can eliminate either a full column (by moving left) or a full row (by moving down). The staircase path emerges naturally from these elimination decisions. Time complexity analysis shows each comparison eliminates either a row or column, so maximum m+n comparisons. Connection to L3: This elimination logic builds upon understanding how to traverse a 2D array systematically. Common mistakes include not recognizing why both sorting properties are necessary, or trying to apply this logic starting from wrong positions.

examples.trace: Current element 7 > target 5. Since column is sorted, all elements below 7 (8,9) are >5. So entire column can be eliminated. Move left to (0,1).

examples.input: At position (1,1)=5 in same matrix, target=6
examples.trace: Current element 5 < target 6. Since row is sorted, all elements left of 5 (4,1) are <6. So entire row can be eliminated. Move down to (2,1).

ladders.title: Building Block — Traversing 2D Arrays with Row/Column Indices
ladders.desc: Master moving through 2D arrays by manipulating row and column indices
ladders.explanation: Before implementing staircase search, you need to be comfortable traversing 2D arrays by manipulating row and column indices. A 2D array (matrix) is typically accessed as matrix[row][col], where row index increases downward and column index increases rightward. The fundamental operations are: incrementing row (move down: row+=1), decrementing row (move up: row-=1), incrementing col (move right: col+=1), and decrementing col (move left: col-=1). In staircase search, we only use two moves: move left (col-=1) and move down (row+=1). It's crucial to understand bounds checking: row must stay within [0, m-1] and col within [0, n-1]. The traversal continues while row < m and col >= 0. Going out of bounds indicates the target is not present. This building block also involves understanding how to start at specific positions (like top-right corner: row=0, col=n-1) and systematically move through the array. Time complexity for simple traversal of entire matrix would be O(m*n), but staircase search achieves O(m+n) by making intelligent moves. Connection to L4: This builds upon basic array indexing operations. Common mistakes include confusing row/col order, incorrect bounds checking, or off-by-one errors in indices. Practice problems include: finding paths in grids, spiral traversal, or diagonal traversal.

examples.input: Start at (0,2) in 3x3 matrix, move left twice, then down once
examples.trace: Start (0,2). Move left: (0,1). Move left: (0,0). Move down: (1,0).

examples.input: Start at (1,1) in 4x4 matrix, move down 2 times, left 1 time
examples.trace: Start (1,1). Move down: (2,1). Move down: (3,1). Move left: (3,0).

testCases.input: Start (0,0) in 2x2 matrix, move right, down
Start (0,3) in 4x4 matrix, move left 2, down 1
Start (2,2) in 3x3 matrix, move up 1, left 1
Start (1,1) in 3x3 matrix, move down 1, right 1
Start (0,4) in 5x5 matrix, move left 3, down 2
Start (3,0) in 4x4 matrix, move right 2, up 1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Elements and Making Decisions
ladders.desc: Compare values and decide next action based on comparison result
ladders.explanation: The most fundamental operation in staircase search is comparing two numbers and making a decision based on the result. Given current element and target, we perform one of three actions: if equal, we found target and stop; if current > target, we move left; if current < target, we move down. This is implemented with simple if-elif-else logic. The intuition is that in a sorted context, comparisons give us information about relative positioning. This operation is performed repeatedly in a loop. It's essential to understand that each comparison reduces the search space. Time complexity for a single comparison is O(1). Space is O(1). Connection to L5: This builds upon basic comparison operators. Common mistakes include using wrong comparison operators (e.g., >= instead of >), not handling equality case separately, or incorrect branching logic. The pseudocode pattern: if current == target: return True; elif current > target: move_left(); else: move_down().

examples.trace: 5 > 3 → current > target → move left
5 < 7 → current < target → move down

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Sorted Arrays and Basic Comparisons
ladders.desc: Learn what sorted means and how to compare numbers using relational operators
ladders.explanation: This is the absolute foundation for staircase search. A sorted array (or matrix) means elements are arranged in increasing order. In a sorted list, if you pick any two elements, the one with smaller index has smaller or equal value. For example, in [1,3,5,7], 1<3<5<7. Basic comparisons use relational operators: == (equal), != (not equal), < (less than), > (greater than), <= (less than or equal), >= (greater than or equal). When comparing two numbers a and b: if a == b, they're equal; if a < b, a is smaller; if a > b, a is larger. In programming, these comparisons return boolean values (true/false). Understanding sorting is crucial because it guarantees properties: in a sorted row, all elements to the right are larger; in a sorted column, all elements below are larger. This foundation is prerequisite for all higher levels. Time complexity for comparison is O(1). Space is O(1). Connection to L4: This basic comparison skill is used repeatedly in the decision logic. Common beginner mistakes include confusing < and >, not understanding sorted order, or incorrect use of equality vs inequality.

examples.trace: 5 is greater than 3, so 5 > 3 returns true

examples.input: In sorted array [2,4,6,8], compare element at index 1 (4) with element at index 3 (8)
examples.trace: Since array is sorted, earlier index has smaller value: index 1 (4) < index 3 (8)
